steps.title: Identify the sentence structure
steps.content: The sentence uses an appositive: a noun phrase that renames the subject. Here "Nigerian expressionist [blank] was known for..." — the blank must supply a name that is part of the subject noun phrase, not set off by commas.

steps.title: Apply the boundary rule for restrictive appositives
steps.content:
Rule: a restrictive appositive (one that identifies which person is meant) takes NO surrounding commas. The name "Gani Odutokun" is essential — without it we don't know which Nigerian expressionist painter. No commas, no period mid-sentence.
  • Choice A — "painter, Gani Odutokun,": two commas make the name non-restrictive, implying only one such painter exists. Incorrect punctuation here. ✗
  • Choice B — "painter Gani Odutokun.": a period creates a sentence fragment — what follows the period has no independent subject. ✗
  • Choice C — "painter Gani Odutokun": no commas, no period — the name sits directly with the noun phrase and the sentence continues correctly. ✓
  • Choice D — "painter, Gani Odutokun": one comma after "painter" disrupts the noun phrase without closing, creating an asymmetric break. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
